Recursive AI raises $300M Series A led by Lightspeed at $2B valuation

Recursive AI, the San Francisco-based startup building autonomous coding agents
for enterprise software teams, announced on Thursday that it has closed a $300
million Series A round led by Lightspeed Venture Partners, with participation
from Founders Fund, Andreessen Horowitz, and existing seed investors. The deal
values the eighteen-month-old company at approximately $2 billion post-money,
according to two sources familiar with the matter.

The round is one of the largest Series A financings of the year and reflects
continued investor appetite for AI infrastructure plays despite a broader
cooling in venture markets. Lightspeed partner Ravi Mhatre, who is joining
Recursive AI's board as part of the transaction, said in a statement that the
firm has been "watching the autonomous-agent category closely for the better
part of a year" and that Recursive AI's approach to long-horizon code
generation was the clearest technical differentiator they had encountered.

Recursive AI was founded in late 2024 by Maya Chen, formerly a research
scientist at Anthropic, and Dan Pereira, who led infrastructure engineering
at Stripe. The company is building what it calls a "coding teammate": an AI
system that operates over multi-day engineering tasks, opening pull requests,
running CI, responding to review comments, and iterating until the work
merges. Unlike single-turn code-completion tools, the system is designed to
hold context across sessions and adapt to a codebase's idiosyncratic
conventions.

Chen said in an interview that the new capital will be used to roughly triple
the engineering headcount over the next twelve months and to expand the
company's go-to-market efforts beyond the small handful of design-partner
customers it has worked with to date. Recursive AI declined to name those
customers but said they include "two of the top five US banks" and "a major
public e-commerce company."

The Series A follows a $24 million seed round announced last March, also led
by Lightspeed with participation from Founders Fund and a syndicate of angel
investors that included Patrick Collison and Nat Friedman. With this round,
Recursive AI has raised approximately $324 million in total equity financing.

Investors and operators in the autonomous-agent space have for months
described the category as bifurcating between general-purpose agent platforms,
which attempt to handle any task a human knowledge worker might perform, and
verticalized systems that pick a single high-value workflow and optimize it
end-to-end. Recursive AI sits squarely in the latter camp. Mhatre cited that
focus as a meaningful factor in Lightspeed's decision: "The teams that win
the agent layer are going to be the ones who can demonstrate measurable
business value in a narrow surface area first, then expand. Recursive picked
a surface — engineering — where the ROI is calculable on day one."

Competing with Recursive AI in the AI-coding agent space are Cognition, the
maker of Devin; Magic, which is building a model purpose-trained for code; and
the agent products from Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google. Several enterprise
software incumbents — most notably GitHub with its Copilot Workspace product
and Atlassian with its Rovo agents — are also moving aggressively into the
category, though their offerings are positioned more as augmentation than
autonomous execution.

Pereira said the company has been deliberate about not releasing a public
self-serve product. "There is a real temptation in this category to chase
the developer-tool playbook of bottoms-up adoption," he said. "But the kinds
of tasks we want our agent to take on — multi-day, high-stakes refactors,
incident response, infrastructure migrations — those are not tasks where you
want a developer just trying us out over the weekend without organizational
buy-in. We're going top-down, slowly, by design."

The company plans to publish a public technical report in the coming weeks
detailing the architecture of its agent system, which Chen described as
combining a custom-trained planning model with a fleet of execution models
specialized for different programming languages and toolchains. Whether the
report will include any benchmarks comparing the system to publicly available
agents is unclear; Chen would say only that "the numbers will speak for
themselves."

Lightspeed's investment in Recursive AI is the firm's third investment in an
AI-coding company in the past eighteen months, following its lead investments
in Codeium and a stealth-mode startup the firm has not publicly named.
Founders Fund partner Trae Stephens, who led that firm's participation in
both the seed and Series A rounds, said Recursive AI's combination of "a
genuinely novel technical approach with the discipline to commercialize
narrowly" was rare in the current market. "Most of what we're seeing is
either great technology with no go-to-market story, or polished
go-to-market wrapped around fairly conventional models. Maya and Dan have
both, and they're moving fast."

The Series A is expected to close in the next two weeks. Recursive AI's
headquarters will remain in San Francisco's Mission District, though the
company plans to open a small New York office to support its East Coast
financial-services customers.
